questions.text: Which of these is correctly classified as a Section 608 "small appliance" requiring only Type I certification?

options.t: A pre-assembled, hermetically sealed window air conditioner factory-charged with 3 lbs of R-410A
A split-system residential heat pump charged with 4 lbs of R-454B, because the charge is under 5 lbs
options.why: Split systems are excluded from the small-appliance definition regardless of charge size — they need Type II, not Type I.

options.t: A 5-ton rooftop package unit, since "package unit" sounds self-contained like a small appliance
options.why: Package units of this size hold far more than 5 lbs and are not factory-sealed small appliances — Type II territory.

options.t: Any appliance a technician chooses to service under an open-book exam
options.why: The open-book format is a feature of the Type I exam delivery, not a definition of what counts as a small appliance.

questions.why: Small appliance = pre-assembled, hermetically sealed, factory-charged with ≤5 lbs of refrigerant (window units, small reach-ins, PTACs, water coolers, etc.). Split systems are excluded no matter how little they are charged with.
questions.ref: EPA 608 Core/Type I definitions; 40 CFR 82 Subpart F

questions.text: Which Section 608 exam section may legally be administered open-book?

options.t: Type I only
Type I and Type II, since both cover lower-charge equipment
options.why: Only Type I gets the open-book allowance. Type II is closed-book like Core and Type III.

options.t: The Core exam, since it is "foundational" material
options.why: Core is closed-book. The open-book exception is specific to Type I, not to any exam labeled "foundational."

options.t: None of them — all four Section 608 exams are closed-book
options.why: Type I is the one documented exception; it may be taken open-book.

questions.why: Type I is the only Section 608 section that certifying organizations may administer open-book. Core, Type II and Type III are closed-book.
questions.ref: EPA-approved certifying organization program rules (ESCO, Mainstream Engineering, etc.)

questions.text: A window air conditioner (small appliance) has reached the end of its life and is headed for disposal. What must happen to its refrigerant first?

options.t: It must be recovered using certified recovery equipment before final disposal
It may be vented, since small appliances fall under a general exemption from the venting prohibition
options.why: There is no blanket small-appliance exemption from the venting prohibition. Refrigerant must still be recovered before the unit is scrapped.

options.t: Nothing — appliance recyclers are responsible for refrigerant, not the servicing technician
options.why: Whoever disposes of (or arranges disposal for) the appliance is responsible for ensuring recovery happens — it does not become someone else's problem by default.

options.t: It must be reclaimed to AHRI 700 purity before disposal
options.why: Reclamation applies when refrigerant changes ownership for reuse. Disposal of a small appliance requires recovery, not a full reclaim cycle.

questions.why: Even small appliances must have refrigerant recovered with certified equipment before final disposal — the venting prohibition applies regardless of appliance category.
questions.ref: 40 CFR 82 Subpart F; EPA 608 Type I disposal requirements

questions.text: Which technician needs ONLY Type I certification (no Type II) to legally service the equipment described?

options.t: A tech who exclusively services small reach-in coolers, window units and dehumidifiers
A tech who services residential split-system heat pumps and mini-splits
options.why: Split systems and mini-splits require Type II regardless of how small their charge is.

options.t: A tech who services rooftop package units on light-commercial buildings
options.why: Rooftop package units are high-pressure appliances above the small-appliance charge/category threshold — Type II applies.

options.t: A tech who services commercial chillers
options.why: Chillers fall under Type II (medium/high-pressure) or Type III (low-pressure), never Type I.

questions.why: Type I alone covers only pre-assembled, hermetically sealed, factory-charged units ≤5 lbs — reach-ins, window units, PTACs, dehumidifiers, water coolers. Anything involving split systems, package units or chillers needs Type II or III.

questions.text: For a small appliance with a working compressor, EPA recovery requirements can be met by recovering what fraction of the refrigerant depending on method?

options.t: 80% or 90% of the charge, depending on whether the compressor is operating and the recovery method used
100% of the charge in all cases, with no exceptions for small appliances
options.why: Small-appliance recovery percentages are 80%/90% depending on compressor operation and method — the rule does not demand a literal 100% pull for small appliances.

options.t: Exactly 50% is sufficient for any small appliance
options.why: 50% is not a recovery standard. The small-appliance figures are 80% (system-dependent, non-operating compressor) or 90% (operating compressor / self-contained).

options.t: Recovery is not required for small appliances at all
options.why: Recovery is required before disposal/service; venting small-appliance refrigerant is illegal.

questions.why: For small appliances, EPA recovery requirements are commonly stated as 80% of the charge (system-dependent equipment with a non-operating compressor) or 90% (self-contained equipment, or when the compressor is operating) — a classic Type I exam point.
questions.ref: EPA 608 Type I recovery requirements; 40 CFR 82.156

questions.text: Which piece of equipment is a "system-dependent" (passive) recovery device best suited for?

options.t: Small appliances, where the appliance's own compressor (if working) or system pressure assists recovery
A 200-lb commercial system with a burned-out compressor
options.why: A dead large system needs self-contained recovery equipment — a passive device has no compressor of its own to pull the charge.

options.t: A low-pressure centrifugal chiller
options.why: Chillers are Type III equipment recovered with dedicated low-pressure recovery gear, not a passive small-appliance device.

options.t: Any appliance, since passive recovery works regardless of compressor condition
options.why: Passive recovery depends on the appliance's own compressor or pressure — it is limited to small appliances / working systems.

questions.why: System-dependent (passive) recovery relies on the appliance's own compressor or internal pressure, so it is limited to small appliances (and working systems). Dead or large systems require self-contained recovery equipment.

questions.text: A technician recovers refrigerant from a household refrigerator into a recovery cylinder and plans to reuse it in another fridge owned by the same customer. What is the minimum processing required?

options.t: It may be reused after recovery (or on-site recycling) as long as it stays with the same owner's equipment
It must be reclaimed to AHRI 700 purity before any reuse, even in the same owner's equipment
options.why: Reclamation is required when refrigerant CHANGES OWNERSHIP. Same-owner reuse can be met with recovery/on-site recycling.

options.t: It can never be reused — recovered refrigerant must always be sent for destruction
options.why: Recovered refrigerant is routinely reused within the same owner's equipment; destruction is not required.

options.t: It must be vented and replaced with virgin refrigerant
options.why: Venting is illegal. Recovered refrigerant is reusable within the same owner's equipment.

questions.why: Recovered (or on-site recycled) refrigerant may be reused within the same owner's equipment without reclamation. Reclamation to AHRI 700 is the trigger only when refrigerant changes ownership.
